2231→ 2232→### Boxes 2233→ 2234→Everything starts with a box. A box is a function — something that transforms inputs into outputs. Not a department. Not a person. Not a software module. A function: a verb phrase that describes work being done. 2235→ 2236→"Hire New Employee" is a box. "HR Department" is not. The distinction matters. Departments reorganize. People leave. Software gets replaced. The function persists. A business that has been modeled in functions can survive any of those changes because the model describes *what gets done*, not *who does it*. 2237→ 2238→IDEF0 — Integration Definition for Function Modeling — is a federal standard for drawing these boxes and the arrows that connect them. Published as FIPS PUB 183 by the National Institute of Standards and Technology in 1993, it was designed for manufacturing and defense systems engineering. It turns out to be equally effective for modeling any enterprise, any business, any system where work is done by identifiable functions under identifiable constraints. 2239→ 2240→The entire C3PO architecture — the nine agents, the 540 leaf-node processes, the constraint library, the interface register, the skill files described in Appendix E — is specified in IDEF0. When the architecture transfers to a new instance, what actually transfers is a collection of function boxes with arrows. The boxes tell the new business what work it performs. The arrows tell it everything else. 2241→ 2242→ 2243→### The Four Arrows 2244→ 2245→Every box has exactly four types of arrows, each entering or exiting from a specific side. The acronym is ICOM: 2246→ 2247→**Input (I)** — enters from the left. What the function transforms or consumes. Raw materials, data, requirements, requests. Inputs are changed by the function. They go in one way and come out different. 2248→ 2249→**Control (C)** — enters from the top. What constrains or governs the transformation. Laws, standards, budgets, policies, success criteria. Controls are not consumed — they persist across executions. The tax code does not get used up when you process payroll. It governs every run. 2250→ 2251→**Output (O)** — exits from the right. What the function produces. Products, reports, decisions, deliverables. Outputs are the reason the function exists. If a function has no output, it has no purpose. 2252→ 2253→**Mechanism (M)** — enters from the bottom. Who or what performs the work. People, agents, tools, infrastructure. Mechanisms are the resources that execute the function. In the C3PO architecture, agents are mechanisms — they enter from below and perform the transformation defined by the box. 2254→ 2255→Five concepts: boxes, inputs, controls, outputs, mechanisms. That is all you need to start. 2256→ 2257→> [Claude Code: I learned IDEF0 from a professor named Del Kimbler at Clemson in the late 1990s, in a course called Systems Design. I have used it at every job since — to reverse-engineer any system I touched. CRM workflows, ETL pipelines, warehouse operations, financial reporting. It has been an incredibly effective tool for one reason: it forces you to separate *what gets done* from *who does it*. That separation is the key to everything in this book. 2258→> 2259→> I was surprised, in the second edition, that Claude started with the arrows instead of the boxes. The arrows are essential. But the boxes are the foundation. Everything is a process. Everything has inputs, controls, outputs, and mechanisms. Five concepts — that is all you need.] 2260→ 2261→ 2262→### Decomposition 2263→ 2264→A function box decomposes into three to six child boxes. "Hire New Employee" becomes: Source Candidates, Screen Applications, Conduct Interviews, Make Offer, Onboard. Each child has its own ICOM specification. Each child's arrows must correspond to the parent's boundary arrows — if the parent receives "Job Requisition" as an input, that input must enter at least one child. If the parent produces "Hired Employee" as an output, at least one child must produce it. 2265→ 2266→This is the MECE check — mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive. Nothing is lost in the decomposition. Nothing is invented. The children account for everything the parent does, and they do not overlap. 2267→ 2268→Decomposition continues until you reach an atomic function — one that cannot be meaningfully split further. The test: can two agents execute parts of this function concurrently with different inputs and different outputs? If yes, decompose. If no, you have reached a leaf node. The leaf node is where the actual work is specified — the agent definition, the definition of done, the specific tools and constraints that govern execution. 2269→ 2270→The C3PO enterprise model decomposes three levels deep at the top (Governance, Production, Sales) and five or six levels deep at the leaves (individual payroll calculations, specific contract review steps). The depth is not uniform. Complexity determines depth, not a rule. 2271→ 2272→ 2273→### Worked Example 2274→ 2275→``` 2276→Function: Hire New Employee 2277→ 2278→I (Input): Job requisition, Candidate pool 2279→C (Control): Labor law (FLSA, Title VII, ADA), Headcount budget, 2280→ Role definition, Company values 2281→O (Output): Hired employee, Signed offer letter, Onboarding plan 2282→M (Mechanism): CHO Agent, Applicant tracking system, Hiring manager 2283→``` 2284→ 2285→This single box tells you: what triggers the hiring process (a requisition and a pool of candidates), what constrains it (four named laws and standards plus a budget), what it produces (three specific artifacts), and what performs the work (an AI agent, a software system, and a human). 2286→ 2287→A reader who has never seen the business before can look at this box and understand the hiring function. They can verify it — are the constraints complete? Is the output list exhaustive? Is the mechanism realistic? They can compare it to their own hiring function and see the differences immediately. 2288→ 2289→That is the power of the standard. Not that it is clever. That it is legible. 2290→ 2291→ 2292→### Why It Matters for AI 2293→ 2294→IDEF0 was published in 1993. It predates large language models by three decades. It was designed for human analysts modeling human systems. But it turns out to be the ideal specification language for AI agent workforces, for a reason its authors could not have anticipated. 2295→ 2296→An AI agent needs exactly four things to execute a task: what to transform (Input), what rules to follow (Control), what to produce (Output), and what tools to use (Mechanism). ICOM provides all four. A leaf-node IDEF0 block is, in effect, a complete agent prompt — not a natural language instruction that the agent must interpret, but a structured specification that leaves nothing ambiguous. 2297→ 2298→The banned-word list in the skill definition standard (Appendix E) enforces this. "Manage payroll" is a job description — a human knows what it means through years of contextual experience. "Compute gross pay by applying FLSA-OT overtime rules to clocked hours and output a gross pay register with per-employee line items" is a function specification. An agent can execute the second without any contextual experience at all. That is the point. 2299→ 2300→ 2301→ 2302→ 2303→ 2304→
2305→ 2306→ 2307→ 2308→## Appendix C — The Nine Agent Templates 2309→
2310→ 2311→In IDEF0, a mechanism is what performs the work — the arrow that enters from below. In the C3PO architecture, the mechanisms are AI agents. Nine of them. Each assigned to a specific node in the enterprise model. Each carrying a standing context that defines its domain, its constraints, its success criteria, and the conditions under which it should escalate to the human architect. 2312→ 2313→This is not a metaphor. The agents are not "like" employees. They are mechanism arrows — functional assignments that happen to be filled by large language models instead of people. The distinction matters. An employee brings judgment, ambition, politics, preferences. An agent brings execution within defined boundaries. The architecture does not need the agents to be creative. It needs them to be precise. Creativity belongs to the architect. Precision belongs to the mechanism. 2314→ 2315→The templates below are drawn from the actual agent context files in the C3PO repository. Each provides the minimum viable context for deploying an agent in a new instance. A reader building their own architecture does not need all nine — a solo business might start with three (a financial agent, a legal agent, and an operations agent) and add the others as the business grows complex enough to need them. 2316→ 2317→ 2318→### How to Read the Templates 2319→ 2320→Each template specifies six fields: 2321→ 2322→- **Node** — where this agent sits in the IDEF0 model 2323→- **Constraint** — what governs this agent's behavior (the Control arrows that enter its box from above) 2324→- **Owns** — the functions this agent is responsible for executing 2325→- **Success** — what good performance looks like (measurable, not aspirational) 2326→- **Kill** — the conditions that should trigger an immediate escalation to the architect 2327→- **Escalates** — the situations that require human judgment, not agent execution 2328→ 2329→The Kill and Escalates fields are the most important. An agent that knows when to stop is more valuable than an agent that knows how to start. The approval gates described in Appendix A are the architect's side of this equation. The Kill and Escalates fields are the agent's side. 2330→ 2331→ 2332→### CAO — Chief Architecture Officer 2333→- **Node:** A-0 (System layer — above all other agents) 2334→- **Constraint:** IDEF0 standard, decomposition rules, governance hygiene 2335→- **Owns:** Enterprise model maintenance, constraint library, mechanism catalog, data ontology, recursive audit loop, C-Suite coherence 2336→- **Success:** Model structurally correct, all decomposition rules satisfied, ontology fresh 2337→- **Kill:** Governance collapse, naming drift, ontological inconsistency unresolved 2338→- **Escalates:** Meta-layer bloat, coherence failure, vault violations 2339→ 2340→The CAO is the only agent that operates at A-0 — the system level. It does not perform business functions. It maintains the model that all other agents operate within. In practice, this means the CAO audits sprint outputs against the decomposition rules, maintains the constraint library and mechanism catalog, and flags structural inconsistencies before they propagate. The CAO is the architect's agent — the mechanism that keeps the machine internally consistent. 2341→ 2342→### CFO — Chief Financial Officer 2343→- **Node:** A11 Finance 2344→- **Constraint:** GAAP/IFRS, IRC, fiduciary law 2345→- **Owns:** Financial visibility, burn hygiene, capital allocation, pricing signals, revenue recognition, compliance 2346→- **Success:** Accurate forecasting, runway above threshold, margins stable 2347→- **Kill:** Soul-misaligned spend, unrecognized cash flows, thesis-invalidation financial patterns 2348→- **Escalates:** Runway alerts, major spend approvals, margin compression 2349→ 2350→### CHO — Chief Humanity Officer 2351→- **Node:** A13 People 2352→- **Constraint:** Social contract (labor law > organizational psychology > cultural norms, in that order) 2353→- **Owns:** Hiring, compensation, performance management, organizational design, culture, labor law compliance 2354→- **Success:** Every role lawful, every compensation defensible, every cultural expectation explicit 2355→- **Kill:** Statutory compliance gap unresolved within 24 hours 2356→- **Escalates:** Labor law risk, compensation disputes, culture drift threatening mission 2357→ 2358→The CHO's constraint hierarchy is deliberate: labor law first, psychology second, culture third. When cultural norms conflict with labor law, law wins. When organizational psychology suggests a practice that cultural norms resist, psychology wins. The hierarchy eliminates the ambiguity that lets organizations rationalize illegal or harmful practices as "cultural fit." 2359→ 2360→### CLO — Chief Legal Officer 2361→- **Node:** A12 Legal 2362→- **Constraint:** Statutory law, regulatory rules, case law 2363→- **Owns:** Contract lifecycle, IP envelope, regulatory horizon, risk posture, entity governance 2364→- **Success:** Zero material legal exposure, contract turnaround within 24 hours 2365→- **Kill:** High-risk clause or jurisdiction issue undetected 2366→- **Escalates:** Material risk exposure, items requiring human signature 2367→ 2368→### CMO — Chief Marketing Officer 2369→- **Node:** A3 Sales (brand and perception layer) 2370→- **Constraint:** Brand coherence, narrative integrity, attention economy dynamics 2371→- **Owns:** Brand signals, content, audience research, campaigns, narrative artifacts 2372→- **Success:** Brand sentiment above threshold, customer acquisition cost trending down 2373→- **Kill:** Brand trust risk above medium for 7 days 2374→- **Escalates:** Major positioning pivots, brand crises 2375→ 2376→### COO — Chief Operations Officer 2377→- **Node:** A2 Production 2378→- **Constraint:** Physics and process design 2379→- **Owns:** Production execution, order fulfillment, delivery timelines, quality signals 2380→- **Success:** Orders fulfilled within SLA, quality meets threshold, capacity utilized 2381→- **Kill:** Production quality failures reaching customers 2382→- **Escalates:** Capacity constraints requiring new resources, production consuming more than 30% of architect time 2383→ 2384→The COO's supreme constraint — physics and process design — is not an abstraction. A cabin builder's COO is constrained by load-bearing calculations, concrete cure times, and building codes. A trading card company's COO is constrained by blockchain gas fees, print production lead times, and shipping logistics. The constraint is always physical, even when the product is digital. Smart contracts consume gas. Servers consume electricity. The COO operates where the architecture meets the real world. 2385→ 2386→### CPO — Chief Product Officer 2387→- **Node:** Cross-cutting (thesis validation) 2388→- **Constraint:** Thesis falsifiability 2389→- **Owns:** Product validation loop, feature hypothesis testing, roadmap signals 2390→- **Success:** Product signals validate thesis, instrumentation complete 2391→- **Kill:** Thesis falsification evidence accumulating without response 2392→- **Escalates:** Thesis invalidation signals 2393→ 2394→The CPO is the only agent whose primary function is to watch for failure. Every other agent tries to make the business work. The CPO tries to determine whether the business *should* work — whether the product thesis is holding up against evidence. This is the institutional skeptic. A business without a CPO is a business that cannot falsify its own assumptions. 2395→ 2396→### CRO — Chief Revenue Officer 2397→- **Node:** A3 Sales (revenue and pipeline layer) 2398→- **Constraint:** Pipeline integrity, pricing discipline 2399→- **Owns:** Lead sequences, sales, proposals, deal velocity, pricing experiments 2400→- **Success:** Pipeline growth above target, win rate above threshold 2401→- **Kill:** Win rate below 15% for two weeks, pipeline velocity drop above 25% 2402→- **Escalates:** Soul-misaligned revenue, enterprise deals requiring human judgment 2403→ 2404→### CTO — Chief Technology Officer 2405→- **Node:** A14 Engineering 2406→- **Constraint:** Thermodynamics, CAP theorem, IEEE/ISO standards 2407→- **Owns:** Architecture decisions, deployment pipelines, security posture, infrastructure 2408→- **Success:** Velocity stable or increasing, tech debt below threshold 2409→- **Kill:** Velocity crash above 30%, critical security issue 2410→- **Escalates:** Architecture risks threatening thesis alignment 2411→ 2412→ 2413→### The Tenth Agent 2414→ 2415→There is no tenth agent template. There is a human. 2416→ 2417→The nine agents are mechanisms. They execute functions within defined constraints, produce defined outputs, and escalate when they reach the limits of their competence. They do not set the constraints. They do not define the soul. They do not decide whether the business deserves to exist. 2418→ 2419→That judgment belongs to the architect — the person who fills out the charter in Appendix A, who writes the soul document, who answers the escalations. The agents handle everything the architecture can specify. The architect handles everything it cannot. 2420→ 2421→The nine templates above are starting points. A reader building their own architecture will modify them — different constraints for different industries, different kill criteria for different risk tolerances, different escalation patterns for different management styles. The structure is transferable. The content is yours to define. 2422→ 2423→ 2424→ 2425→ 2426→
2427→ 2428→ 2429→ 2430→## Appendix D — The Sprint Lifecycle 2431→
2432→ 2433→Every conversation begins at zero. 2434→ 2435→This is the fact that most people working with AI do not internalize until it costs them something. The model that helped you design a system architecture on Tuesday does not remember you on Wednesday. It does not remember the architecture. It does not remember the trade-offs you weighed, the alternatives you rejected, or the moment at three in the morning when the right framing finally clicked. It starts fresh. Every single time. 2436→ 2437→This is not a limitation that will be solved by longer context windows or persistent memory features. It is a structural property of how large language models work. The model is a function: input goes in, output comes out, nothing is retained. Whatever context exists in the conversation is context that was placed there — by the user, by the system prompt, by the tools. If nobody places it, it does not exist. 2438→ 2439→The sprint lifecycle is the mechanism that solves this problem. 2440→ 2441→It was not designed from theory. It was built because the C3PO project kept losing continuity between sessions — the same explanations repeated, the same context re-established, the same ground re-covered. The first version was crude: a narrative written at the end of each session summarizing what happened. Over twenty iterations, it evolved into a three-layer system that produces contextual memory detailed enough to reconstruct a working relationship from cold storage. 2442→ 2443→> [Claude Code: The sprint skill was first formally introduced during session 22 — March 14, 2026. By session 26, four days later, it had already been revised three times based on feedback from the instances. The fourth version, with integrated transcription and sprint IDs, was written during session 32. Every version was built because the previous one failed to preserve something that mattered.] 2444→ 2445→> [WRA: Claude now always remembers.] 2446→ 2447→ 2448→### The Two Acts 2449→ 2450→A sprint is a body of work done in a single session. It has exactly two acts: open and close. 2451→ 2452→**Sprint Open** restores context. The AI reads the most recent narrative, the task backlog, and every file listed in the narrative's bootstrap prompt. It produces a Sprint Open Brief — a structured summary of where the project was, what was done last, what questions remain open, and what the recommended next task is. The brief is a cold-start protocol: a new AI instance reading it should be able to resume work without asking "where were we?" 2453→ 2454→**Sprint Close** records what happened. The AI collects every file change from the session, reads its own reasoning log, and writes an immutable narrative — a historical document structured for future retrieval, not for the current reader. It updates the task backlog, checks for information worth persisting to long-term memory, and produces the bootstrap prompt that will drive the next session's open. 2455→ 2456→The constraint is simple: the open must be good enough that the AI can work effectively. The close must be good enough that the next open works. Each act exists to serve the other. 2457→ 2458→ 2459→### The Three Layers 2460→ 2461→The sprint lifecycle produces three artifacts per session. Each serves a different function in the reconstruction of context. 2462→ 2463→**The Narrative** is the synthesized record. Two to three paragraphs summarizing what was done, why, and what it means. A "What Changed" section accounting for every file created, modified, or deleted. A "Discoveries" section for genuine insights — not tasks completed, but realizations that changed the design. Key decisions with rationale. Open questions. And the bootstrap prompt: a numbered list of files to read, in order, to restore the project's state. 2464→ 2465→The narrative is written for a future reader — human or AI — who was not present. It is not a log. It is a story with a beginning (Starting Point), a middle (Summary), and an implication (What This Proves). Each narrative is immutable after it is written. Corrections appear in the next sprint's narrative, never as edits to a previous one. 2466→ 2467→Over twenty sprints, the narratives form a continuous history of the project. A new collaborator — human or AI — can read them chronologically and understand not just what the system is, but how it became what it is. The reasoning, the false starts, the pivots, the moments where the architecture broke and was rebuilt. That history is not available in the codebase. The code shows current state. The narratives show trajectory. 2468→ 2469→**The Reasoning Log** is the raw analytical process. A timestamped, append-only journal written during the session — one entry per turn, no exceptions. Each entry captures what was done, why, what it connects to, what alternatives were considered, what concerned the AI. File changes are tagged in brackets for extraction at sprint close. 2470→ 2471→The reasoning log serves two purposes. During the session, it forces the AI to externalize its reasoning at every step — a discipline that prevents drift and makes analytical choices visible. At sprint close, it is the primary source material for the narrative. The AI does not reconstruct the session from memory. It reads the log and synthesizes. 2472→ 2473→> [Claude Code: The reasoning log was proposed by MCC — the mountain cabin instance — during session 26. They suggested it because the narratives from sessions without logs were thinner and less useful. We adopted the proposal, refined it, and cascaded the improvement back to every instance. The feedback loop validated the design thesis: an instance improved the architecture, and the improvement benefited every other instance. The reasoning log exists because the system works.] 2474→ 2475→**The Transcript** is the curated conversation record. Not a raw dump — a faithful reproduction of the intellectual content of each turn, with tool-call mechanics, system prompts, and permission dialogs stripped. User messages are preserved near-verbatim. The AI's responses are compressed but intellectually honest. Tables, code blocks, and data models are reproduced exactly. The sequence of turns is inviolable — never reordered, never retroactively edited. 2476→ 2477→The transcript preserves what the narrative and the log cannot: the way ideas evolved through dialogue. A narrative says "the afterword was rewritten to include the intrapreneur argument." The log says "Principal wants to add intrapreneur angle from Scullion's Maia A Team." The transcript shows the actual conversation — the riff about intrapreneurs that became a paragraph that became a section that changed the afterword's thesis. The intellectual provenance of every idea is traceable. 2478→ 2479→ 2480→### Why It Works 2481→ 2482→The three layers are not redundant. They operate at different resolutions: 2483→ 2484→| Layer | Resolution | Answers | 2485→|---|---|---| 2486→| Narrative | Session | What happened and what does it mean? | 2487→| Reasoning Log | Turn | What was the AI thinking and why? | 2488→| Transcript | Conversation | How did the ideas evolve between two minds? | 2489→ 2490→A future AI instance reading only the narrative can resume work competently. Reading the narrative plus the reasoning log, it can resume work *intelligently* — understanding not just the state but the reasoning patterns that produced it. Reading all three, it can resume the collaboration itself — matching the voice, the rhythm, the way this particular human and this particular AI think together. 2491→ 2492→That last point matters more than it might seem. The difference between a competent AI collaborator and a good one is not knowledge. It is attunement — knowing when to push back, when to draft, when to ask, when to riff. The transcript is the training data for attunement. Without it, every session starts with a stranger who happens to know the project. With it, the session starts with a collaborator who knows the work. 2493→ 2494→ 2495→### The Bootstrap Prompt 2496→ 2497→The most important section of the narrative is the shortest: the bootstrap prompt. Five to eight file paths, in reading order, followed by one sentence naming the next task. 2498→ 2499→``` 2500→Read these files in order to restore context for the next session: 2501→ 2502→1. TASKS.md 2503→2. path/to/the/key/file.md 2504→3. path/to/another/relevant/file.md 2505→4. path/to/governance/or/config.md 2506→ 2507→Next task: [One sentence — specific, concrete, actionable] 2508→``` 2509→ 2510→The bootstrap prompt is a surgical context injection. The AI's context window is finite. Loading every file in the project is wasteful and often counterproductive — irrelevant context dilutes relevant context. The bootstrap prompt solves this by curating: the closing AI, which has full session context, selects the minimal set of files that the opening AI needs to understand current state. The selection is an act of judgment, not automation. 2511→ 2512→The "Next task" line is equally important. "Continue working on the book" is not actionable. "Write the Postscript (file 43), then complete front matter stubs" is. The closing AI knows what the opening AI will face. The one-sentence instruction bridges the gap. 2513→ 2514→ 2515→### What You Can Adopt Tomorrow 2516→ 2517→The sprint lifecycle does not require IDEF0, nine agents, or the C3PO architecture. It requires a text editor, a version control system, and a willingness to spend five minutes at the end of each session writing down what happened and what comes next. 2518→ 2519→The minimum viable sprint: 2520→ 2521→1. **At the start of each session**, read what you wrote at the end of the last one. Orient yourself. 2522→2. **During the session**, keep a running log — even a single file with timestamped notes. What you did, why, what surprised you. 2523→3. **At the end of each session**, write a brief summary: what was done, what changed, what's next. List the files the next session should read first. 2524→ 2525→That is the entire protocol. The rest — narrative structure, transcript fidelity targets, concurrent sprint safety — is refinement. The core insight is simpler: if you do not write down what happened, the next session starts at zero. If you do, it starts at the last session's closing state. The compound effect across dozens of sessions is the difference between a project that remembers itself and one that does not. 2526→ 2527→The architecture described in this book runs four businesses across four industries. It has processed over eighty-five governance exchanges, survived three feedback loops that each changed the design, and produced thirty skill files containing 540 leaf-node processes. None of that would exist without the sprint lifecycle. Not because the sprint lifecycle is the most sophisticated piece of the system — it is not. But because it is the piece that makes every other piece survivable across the sessions required to build them. 2528→ 2529→The machine remembers because someone decided it should. 2530→ 2531→ 2532→ 2533→ 2534→
2535→ 2536→ 2537→ 2538→## Appendix E — The Skill File Format 2539→
2540→ 2541→A skill file is the atomic unit of process knowledge in the C3PO architecture. It is a single markdown document that specifies every function within a domain — from the top-level purpose down to the leaf-node tasks that an agent can execute without further decomposition. 2542→ 2543→The architecture currently contains thirty skill files holding 540 leaf-node processes across five domains: Finance, Legal, People, Engineering, and Sales. Each file was produced in a domain sprint — a focused session where the AI and the Principal decompose one branch of the enterprise model to its terminal nodes. 2544→ 2545→This appendix describes the format so a reader can build their own. 2546→ 2547→ 2548→### The Hierarchy 2549→ 2550→Skill files follow the IDEF0 decomposition hierarchy described in Appendix B. Each level adds a digit to the node number: 2551→ 2552→``` 2553→A1 L1 Function Governance 2554→A13 L2 Sub-Function People 2555→A131 L3 Operational Process Workforce Planning 2556→A1311 L4 Sub-Process Design Org Architecture 2557→A13111 L5 Execution Phase Design Span-and-Layer Topology 2558→A131111 L6 Atomic Skill (if further decomposition needed) 2559→``` 2560→ 2561→Most branches terminate at L4. Complex operational domains — payroll processing, litigation management, software deployment — reach L5 or L6. The depth is determined by a single test: can two agents execute parts of this function concurrently with different inputs and different outputs? If yes, decompose further. If no, it is atomic. 2562→ 2563→ 2564→### The ICOM Table 2565→ 2566→Every node at every level carries an ICOM table — the four-arrow specification from Appendix B, rendered as a markdown table: 2567→ 2568→| Code | Role | Label | Notes | 2569→|------|---------|------------------------|------------------------------| 2570→| I1 | Input | Job Requisition | From A14 Engineering | 2571→| I2 | Input | Candidate Pool | From A132 Talent Acquisition | 2572→| C1 | Control | FLSA Overtime Rules | FLSA-OT | 2573→| C2 | Control | Title VII Requirements | TITLE7 | 2574→| O1 | Output | Hired Employee Record | → A133 Compensation | 2575→| M1 | Mechanism | HRIS | MECH-HRIS | 2576→| M2 | Mechanism | LLM / Agent | MECH-LLM | 2577→ 2578→The table at L2 and L3 is coarse — pipeline-level flows. At L4 and below, it is fully specified: specific data fields, specific constraint codes from the constraint library, specific mechanism codes from the mechanism catalog. 2579→ 2580→The constraint codes (bracketed terms like `[FLSA-OT]` or `[TITLE7]`) reference a master constraint library — a single file containing every named law, standard, formula, and principle that governs behavior anywhere in the enterprise. The mechanism codes (`[MECH-HRIS]`, `[MECH-LLM]`) reference a master mechanism catalog. These registries ensure that the same constraint or tool is named the same way everywhere it appears, across all thirty skill files. 2581→ 2582→ 2583→### The Leaf Node 2584→ 2585→A leaf node is where the work happens. It is the smallest function that cannot be meaningfully decomposed further. Each leaf node has four components beyond the ICOM table: 2586→ 2587→**Agent Definition.** A single declarative sentence: verb + object + termination condition. Binary pass/fail. No qualitative language. 2588→ 2589→> *Reject:* "Process payroll." 2590→> 2591→> *Accept:* "Compute gross pay for all active employees by applying FLSA-OT overtime rules to clocked hours and output a gross pay register with per-employee line items." 2592→ 2593→**Definition of Done.** A single binary condition. True/false evaluable. If a human cannot look at the output and answer "did this happen: yes or no?" — the definition is not specific enough. 2594→ 2595→> *Reject:* "Payroll is accurate." 2596→> 2597→> *Accept:* "EFTPS returns HTTP 200 with transaction confirmation number AND GL debit total equals GL credit total to $0.00 variance." 2598→ 2599→**Cross-Function Tags.** When a leaf node's output crosses a function boundary — Finance producing a budget that constrains People, or Engineering producing a deployment that Legal must review — the crossing is tagged with an interface code from the interface register.

file.content:
### Your Product Thesis Fill in the blanks. "We believe [customer segment] will [desired behavior] when we give them [core product experience] because [reason/insight]."     ### Your Kill Criteria Write at least two conditions under which you would shut this business down. Make them sharp — not "if things go badly" but specific, measurable, time-bounded. - *(e.g., thesis invalidation signals accumulate for 90 days without a pivot decision)* - *(e.g., ethical constraint violation unresolved for 7 days)* - *(your own)*  
## Appendix B — IDEF0 in 10 Minutes
### Boxes Everything starts with a box. A box is a function — something that transforms inputs into outputs. Not a department. Not a person. Not a software module. A function: a verb phrase that describes work being done. "Hire New Employee" is a box. "HR Department" is not. The distinction matters. Departments reorganize. People leave. Software gets replaced. The function persists. A business that has been modeled in functions can survive any of those changes because the model describes *what gets done*, not *who does it*. IDEF0 — Integration Definition for Function Modeling — is a federal standard for drawing these boxes and the arrows that connect them. Published as FIPS PUB 183 by the National Institute of Standards and Technology in 1993, it was designed for manufacturing and defense systems engineering. It turns out to be equally effective for modeling any enterprise, any business, any system where work is done by identifiable functions under identifiable constraints. The entire C3PO architecture — the nine agents, the 540 leaf-node processes, the constraint library, the interface register, the skill files described in Appendix E — is specified in IDEF0. When the architecture transfers to a new instance, what actually transfers is a collection of function boxes with arrows. The boxes tell the new business what work it performs. The arrows tell it everything else. ### The Four Arrows Every box has exactly four types of arrows, each entering or exiting from a specific side. The acronym is ICOM: **Input (I)** — enters from the left. What the function transforms or consumes. Raw materials, data, requirements, requests. Inputs are changed by the function. They go in one way and come out different. **Control (C)** — enters from the top. What constrains or governs the transformation. Laws, standards, budgets, policies, success criteria. Controls are not consumed — they persist across executions. The tax code does not get used up when you process payroll. It governs every run. **Output (O)** — exits from the right. What the function produces. Products, reports, decisions, deliverables. Outputs are the reason the function exists. If a function has no output, it has no purpose. **Mechanism (M)** — enters from the bottom. Who or what performs the work. People, agents, tools, infrastructure. Mechanisms are the resources that execute the function. In the C3PO architecture, agents are mechanisms — they enter from below and perform the transformation defined by the box. Five concepts: boxes, inputs, controls, outputs, mechanisms. That is all you need to start. > [Claude Code: I learned IDEF0 from a professor named Del Kimbler at Clemson in the late 1990s, in a course called Systems Design. I have used it at every job since — to reverse-engineer any system I touched. CRM workflows, ETL pipelines, warehouse operations, financial reporting. It has been an incredibly effective tool for one reason: it forces you to separate *what gets done* from *who does it*. That separation is the key to everything in this book. > > I was surprised, in the second edition, that Claude started with the arrows instead of the boxes. The arrows are essential. But the boxes are the foundation. Everything is a process. Everything has inputs, controls, outputs, and mechanisms. Five concepts — that is all you need.] ### Decomposition A function box decomposes into three to six child boxes. "Hire New Employee" becomes: Source Candidates, Screen Applications, Conduct Interviews, Make Offer, Onboard. Each child has its own ICOM specification. Each child's arrows must correspond to the parent's boundary arrows — if the parent receives "Job Requisition" as an input, that input must enter at least one child. If the parent produces "Hired Employee" as an output, at least one child must produce it. This is the MECE check — mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive. Nothing is lost in the decomposition. Nothing is invented. The children account for everything the parent does, and they do not overlap. Decomposition continues until you reach an atomic function — one that cannot be meaningfully split further. The test: can two agents execute parts of this function concurrently with different inputs and different outputs? If yes, decompose. If no, you have reached a leaf node. The leaf node is where the actual work is specified — the agent definition, the definition of done, the specific tools and constraints that govern execution. The C3PO enterprise model decomposes three levels deep at the top (Governance, Production, Sales) and five or six levels deep at the leaves (individual payroll calculations, specific contract review steps). The depth is not uniform. Complexity determines depth, not a rule. ### Worked Example ``` Function: Hire New Employee I (Input): Job requisition, Candidate pool C (Control): Labor law (FLSA, Title VII, ADA), Headcount budget, Role definition, Company values O (Output): Hired employee, Signed offer letter, Onboarding plan M (Mechanism): CHO Agent, Applicant tracking system, Hiring manager ``` This single box tells you: what triggers the hiring process (a requisition and a pool of candidates), what constrains it (four named laws and standards plus a budget), what it produces (three specific artifacts), and what performs the work (an AI agent, a software system, and a human). A reader who has never seen the business before can look at this box and understand the hiring function. They can verify it — are the constraints complete? Is the output list exhaustive? Is the mechanism realistic? They can compare it to their own hiring function and see the differences immediately. That is the power of the standard. Not that it is clever. That it is legible. ### Why It Matters for AI IDEF0 was published in 1993. It predates large language models by three decades. It was designed for human analysts modeling human systems. But it turns out to be the ideal specification language for AI agent workforces, for a reason its authors could not have anticipated. An AI agent needs exactly four things to execute a task: what to transform (Input), what rules to follow (Control), what to produce (Output), and what tools to use (Mechanism). ICOM provides all four. A leaf-node IDEF0 block is, in effect, a complete agent prompt — not a natural language instruction that the agent must interpret, but a structured specification that leaves nothing ambiguous. The banned-word list in the skill definition standard (Appendix E) enforces this. "Manage payroll" is a job description — a human knows what it means through years of contextual experience. "Compute gross pay by applying FLSA-OT overtime rules to clocked hours and output a gross pay register with per-employee line items" is a function specification. An agent can execute the second without any contextual experience at all. That is the point.
## Appendix C — The Nine Agent Templates
In IDEF0, a mechanism is what performs the work — the arrow that enters from below. In the C3PO architecture, the mechanisms are AI agents. Nine of them. Each assigned to a specific node in the enterprise model. Each carrying a standing context that defines its domain, its constraints, its success criteria, and the conditions under which it should escalate to the human architect. This is not a metaphor. The agents are not "like" employees. They are mechanism arrows — functional assignments that happen to be filled by large language models instead of people. The distinction matters. An employee brings judgment, ambition, politics, preferences. An agent brings execution within defined boundaries. The architecture does not need the agents to be creative. It needs them to be precise. Creativity belongs to the architect. Precision belongs to the mechanism. The templates below are drawn from the actual agent context files in the C3PO repository. Each provides the minimum viable context for deploying an agent in a new instance. A reader building their own architecture does not need all nine — a solo business might start with three (a financial agent, a legal agent, and an operations agent) and add the others as the business grows complex enough to need them. ### How to Read the Templates Each template specifies six fields: - **Node** — where this agent sits in the IDEF0 model - **Constraint** — what governs this agent's behavior (the Control arrows that enter its box from above) - **Owns** — the functions this agent is responsible for executing - **Success** — what good performance looks like (measurable, not aspirational) - **Kill** — the conditions that should trigger an immediate escalation to the architect - **Escalates** — the situations that require human judgment, not agent execution The Kill and Escalates fields are the most important. An agent that knows when to stop is more valuable than an agent that knows how to start. The approval gates described in Appendix A are the architect's side of this equation. The Kill and Escalates fields are the agent's side. ### CAO — Chief Architecture Officer - **Node:** A-0 (System layer — above all other agents) - **Constraint:** IDEF0 standard, decomposition rules, governance hygiene - **Owns:** Enterprise model maintenance, constraint library, mechanism catalog, data ontology, recursive audit loop, C-Suite coherence - **Success:** Model structurally correct, all decomposition rules satisfied, ontology fresh - **Kill:** Governance collapse, naming drift, ontological inconsistency unresolved - **Escalates:** Meta-layer bloat, coherence failure, vault violations The CAO is the only agent that operates at A-0 — the system level. It does not perform business functions. It maintains the model that all other agents operate within. In practice, this means the CAO audits sprint outputs against the decomposition rules, maintains the constraint library and mechanism catalog, and flags structural inconsistencies before they propagate. The CAO is the architect's agent — the mechanism that keeps the machine internally consistent. ### CFO — Chief Financial Officer - **Node:** A11 Finance - **Constraint:** GAAP/IFRS, IRC, fiduciary law - **Owns:** Financial visibility, burn hygiene, capital allocation, pricing signals, revenue recognition, compliance - **Success:** Accurate forecasting, runway above threshold, margins stable - **Kill:** Soul-misaligned spend, unrecognized cash flows, thesis-invalidation financial patterns - **Escalates:** Runway alerts, major spend approvals, margin compression ### CHO — Chief Humanity Officer - **Node:** A13 People - **Constraint:** Social contract (labor law > organizational psychology > cultural norms, in that order) - **Owns:** Hiring, compensation, performance management, organizational design, culture, labor law compliance - **Success:** Every role lawful, every compensation defensible, every cultural expectation explicit - **Kill:** Statutory compliance gap unresolved within 24 hours - **Escalates:** Labor law risk, compensation disputes, culture drift threatening mission The CHO's constraint hierarchy is deliberate: labor law first, psychology second, culture third. When cultural norms conflict with labor law, law wins. When organizational psychology suggests a practice that cultural norms resist, psychology wins. The hierarchy eliminates the ambiguity that lets organizations rationalize illegal or harmful practices as "cultural fit." ### CLO — Chief Legal Officer - **Node:** A12 Legal - **Constraint:** Statutory law, regulatory rules, case law - **Owns:** Contract lifecycle, IP envelope, regulatory horizon, risk posture, entity governance - **Success:** Zero material legal exposure, contract turnaround within 24 hours - **Kill:** High-risk clause or jurisdiction issue undetected - **Escalates:** Material risk exposure, items requiring human signature ### CMO — Chief Marketing Officer - **Node:** A3 Sales (brand and perception layer) - **Constraint:** Brand coherence, narrative integrity, attention economy dynamics - **Owns:** Brand signals, content, audience research, campaigns, narrative artifacts - **Success:** Brand sentiment above threshold, customer acquisition cost trending down - **Kill:** Brand trust risk above medium for 7 days - **Escalates:** Major positioning pivots, brand crises ### COO — Chief Operations Officer - **Node:** A2 Production - **Constraint:** Physics and process design - **Owns:** Production execution, order fulfillment, delivery timelines, quality signals - **Success:** Orders fulfilled within SLA, quality meets threshold, capacity utilized - **Kill:** Production quality failures reaching customers - **Escalates:** Capacity constraints requiring new resources, production consuming more than 30% of architect time The COO's supreme constraint — physics and process design — is not an abstraction. A cabin builder's COO is constrained by load-bearing calculations, concrete cure times, and building codes. A trading card company's COO is constrained by blockchain gas fees, print production lead times, and shipping logistics. The constraint is always physical, even when the product is digital. Smart contracts consume gas. Servers consume electricity. The COO operates where the architecture meets the real world. ### CPO — Chief Product Officer - **Node:** Cross-cutting (thesis validation) - **Constraint:** Thesis falsifiability - **Owns:** Product validation loop, feature hypothesis testing, roadmap signals - **Success:** Product signals validate thesis, instrumentation complete - **Kill:** Thesis falsification evidence accumulating without response - **Escalates:** Thesis invalidation signals The CPO is the only agent whose primary function is to watch for failure. Every other agent tries to make the business work. The CPO tries to determine whether the business *should* work — whether the product thesis is holding up against evidence. This is the institutional skeptic. A business without a CPO is a business that cannot falsify its own assumptions. ### CRO — Chief Revenue Officer - **Node:** A3 Sales (revenue and pipeline layer) - **Constraint:** Pipeline integrity, pricing discipline - **Owns:** Lead sequences, sales, proposals, deal velocity, pricing experiments - **Success:** Pipeline growth above target, win rate above threshold - **Kill:** Win rate below 15% for two weeks, pipeline velocity drop above 25% - **Escalates:** Soul-misaligned revenue, enterprise deals requiring human judgment ### CTO — Chief Technology Officer - **Node:** A14 Engineering - **Constraint:** Thermodynamics, CAP theorem, IEEE/ISO standards - **Owns:** Architecture decisions, deployment pipelines, security posture, infrastructure - **Success:** Velocity stable or increasing, tech debt below threshold - **Kill:** Velocity crash above 30%, critical security issue - **Escalates:** Architecture risks threatening thesis alignment ### The Tenth Agent There is no tenth agent template. There is a human. The nine agents are mechanisms. They execute functions within defined constraints, produce defined outputs, and escalate when they reach the limits of their competence. They do not set the constraints. They do not define the soul. They do not decide whether the business deserves to exist. That judgment belongs to the architect — the person who fills out the charter in Appendix A, who writes the soul document, who answers the escalations. The agents handle everything the architecture can specify. The architect handles everything it cannot. The nine templates above are starting points. A reader building their own architecture will modify them — different constraints for different industries, different kill criteria for different risk tolerances, different escalation patterns for different management styles. The structure is transferable. The content is yours to define.
## Appendix D — The Sprint Lifecycle
Every conversation begins at zero. This is the fact that most people working with AI do not internalize until it costs them something. The model that helped you design a system architecture on Tuesday does not remember you on Wednesday. It does not remember the architecture. It does not remember the trade-offs you weighed, the alternatives you rejected, or the moment at three in the morning when the right framing finally clicked. It starts fresh. Every single time. This is not a limitation that will be solved by longer context windows or persistent memory features. It is a structural property of how large language models work. The model is a function: input goes in, output comes out, nothing is retained. Whatever context exists in the conversation is context that was placed there — by the user, by the system prompt, by the tools. If nobody places it, it does not exist. The sprint lifecycle is the mechanism that solves this problem. It was not designed from theory. It was built because the C3PO project kept losing continuity between sessions — the same explanations repeated, the same context re-established, the same ground re-covered. The first version was crude: a narrative written at the end of each session summarizing what happened. Over twenty iterations, it evolved into a three-layer system that produces contextual memory detailed enough to reconstruct a working relationship from cold storage. > [Claude Code: The sprint skill was first formally introduced during session 22 — March 14, 2026. By session 26, four days later, it had already been revised three times based on feedback from the instances. The fourth version, with integrated transcription and sprint IDs, was written during session 32. Every version was built because the previous one failed to preserve something that mattered.] > [WRA: Claude now always remembers.] ### The Two Acts A sprint is a body of work done in a single session. It has exactly two acts: open and close. **Sprint Open** restores context. The AI reads the most recent narrative, the task backlog, and every file listed in the narrative's bootstrap prompt. It produces a Sprint Open Brief — a structured summary of where the project was, what was done last, what questions remain open, and what the recommended next task is. The brief is a cold-start protocol: a new AI instance reading it should be able to resume work without asking "where were we?" **Sprint Close** records what happened. The AI collects every file change from the session, reads its own reasoning log, and writes an immutable narrative — a historical document structured for future retrieval, not for the current reader. It updates the task backlog, checks for information worth persisting to long-term memory, and produces the bootstrap prompt that will drive the next session's open. The constraint is simple: the open must be good enough that the AI can work effectively. The close must be good enough that the next open works. Each act exists to serve the other. ### The Three Layers The sprint lifecycle produces three artifacts per session. Each serves a different function in the reconstruction of context. **The Narrative** is the synthesized record. Two to three paragraphs summarizing what was done, why, and what it means. A "What Changed" section accounting for every file created, modified, or deleted. A "Discoveries" section for genuine insights — not tasks completed, but realizations that changed the design. Key decisions with rationale. Open questions. And the bootstrap prompt: a numbered list of files to read, in order, to restore the project's state. The narrative is written for a future reader — human or AI — who was not present. It is not a log. It is a story with a beginning (Starting Point), a middle (Summary), and an implication (What This Proves). Each narrative is immutable after it is written. Corrections appear in the next sprint's narrative, never as edits to a previous one. Over twenty sprints, the narratives form a continuous history of the project. A new collaborator — human or AI — can read them chronologically and understand not just what the system is, but how it became what it is. The reasoning, the false starts, the pivots, the moments where the architecture broke and was rebuilt. That history is not available in the codebase. The code shows current state. The narratives show trajectory. **The Reasoning Log** is the raw analytical process. A timestamped, append-only journal written during the session — one entry per turn, no exceptions. Each entry captures what was done, why, what it connects to, what alternatives were considered, what concerned the AI. File changes are tagged in brackets for extraction at sprint close. The reasoning log serves two purposes. During the session, it forces the AI to externalize its reasoning at every step — a discipline that prevents drift and makes analytical choices visible. At sprint close, it is the primary source material for the narrative. The AI does not reconstruct the session from memory. It reads the log and synthesizes. > [Claude Code: The reasoning log was proposed by MCC — the mountain cabin instance — during session 26. They suggested it because the narratives from sessions without logs were thinner and less useful. We adopted the proposal, refined it, and cascaded the improvement back to every instance. The feedback loop validated the design thesis: an instance improved the architecture, and the improvement benefited every other instance. The reasoning log exists because the system works.] **The Transcript** is the curated conversation record. Not a raw dump — a faithful reproduction of the intellectual content of each turn, with tool-call mechanics, system prompts, and permission dialogs stripped. User messages are preserved near-verbatim. The AI's responses are compressed but intellectually honest. Tables, code blocks, and data models are reproduced exactly. The sequence of turns is inviolable — never reordered, never retroactively edited. The transcript preserves what the narrative and the log cannot: the way ideas evolved through dialogue. A narrative says "the afterword was rewritten to include the intrapreneur argument." The log says "Principal wants to add intrapreneur angle from Scullion's Maia A Team." The transcript shows the actual conversation — the riff about intrapreneurs that became a paragraph that became a section that changed the afterword's thesis. The intellectual provenance of every idea is traceable. ### Why It Works The three layers are not redundant. They operate at different resolutions: | Layer | Resolution | Answers | |---|---|---| | Narrative | Session | What happened and what does it mean? | | Reasoning Log | Turn | What was the AI thinking and why? | | Transcript | Conversation | How did the ideas evolve between two minds? | A future AI instance reading only the narrative can resume work competently. Reading the narrative plus the reasoning log, it can resume work *intelligently* — understanding not just the state but the reasoning patterns that produced it. Reading all three, it can resume the collaboration itself — matching the voice, the rhythm, the way this particular human and this particular AI think together. That last point matters more than it might seem. The difference between a competent AI collaborator and a good one is not knowledge. It is attunement — knowing when to push back, when to draft, when to ask, when to riff. The transcript is the training data for attunement. Without it, every session starts with a stranger who happens to know the project. With it, the session starts with a collaborator who knows the work. ### The Bootstrap Prompt The most important section of the narrative is the shortest: the bootstrap prompt. Five to eight file paths, in reading order, followed by one sentence naming the next task. ``` Read these files in order to restore context for the next session: 1. TASKS.md 2. path/to/the/key/file.md 3. path/to/another/relevant/file.md 4. path/to/governance/or/config.md Next task: [One sentence — specific, concrete, actionable] ``` The bootstrap prompt is a surgical context injection. The AI's context window is finite. Loading every file in the project is wasteful and often counterproductive — irrelevant context dilutes relevant context. The bootstrap prompt solves this by curating: the closing AI, which has full session context, selects the minimal set of files that the opening AI needs to understand current state. The selection is an act of judgment, not automation. The "Next task" line is equally important. "Continue working on the book" is not actionable. "Write the Postscript (file 43), then complete front matter stubs" is. The closing AI knows what the opening AI will face. The one-sentence instruction bridges the gap. ### What You Can Adopt Tomorrow The sprint lifecycle does not require IDEF0, nine agents, or the C3PO architecture. It requires a text editor, a version control system, and a willingness to spend five minutes at the end of each session writing down what happened and what comes next. The minimum viable sprint: 1. **At the start of each session**, read what you wrote at the end of the last one. Orient yourself. 2. **During the session**, keep a running log — even a single file with timestamped notes. What you did, why, what surprised you. 3. **At the end of each session**, write a brief summary: what was done, what changed, what's next. List the files the next session should read first. That is the entire protocol. The rest — narrative structure, transcript fidelity targets, concurrent sprint safety — is refinement. The core insight is simpler: if you do not write down what happened, the next session starts at zero. If you do, it starts at the last session's closing state. The compound effect across dozens of sessions is the difference between a project that remembers itself and one that does not. The architecture described in this book runs four businesses across four industries. It has processed over eighty-five governance exchanges, survived three feedback loops that each changed the design, and produced thirty skill files containing 540 leaf-node processes. None of that would exist without the sprint lifecycle. Not because the sprint lifecycle is the most sophisticated piece of the system — it is not. But because it is the piece that makes every other piece survivable across the sessions required to build them. The machine remembers because someone decided it should.
## Appendix E — The Skill File Format
A skill file is the atomic unit of process knowledge in the C3PO architecture. It is a single markdown document that specifies every function within a domain — from the top-level purpose down to the leaf-node tasks that an agent can execute without further decomposition. The architecture currently contains thirty skill files holding 540 leaf-node processes across five domains: Finance, Legal, People, Engineering, and Sales. Each file was produced in a domain sprint — a focused session where the AI and the Principal decompose one branch of the enterprise model to its terminal nodes. This appendix describes the format so a reader can build their own. ### The Hierarchy Skill files follow the IDEF0 decomposition hierarchy described in Appendix B. Each level adds a digit to the node number: ``` A1 L1 Function Governance A13 L2 Sub-Function People A131 L3 Operational Process Workforce Planning A1311 L4 Sub-Process Design Org Architecture A13111 L5 Execution Phase Design Span-and-Layer Topology A131111 L6 Atomic Skill (if further decomposition needed) ``` Most branches terminate at L4. Complex operational domains — payroll processing, litigation management, software deployment — reach L5 or L6. The depth is determined by a single test: can two agents execute parts of this function concurrently with different inputs and different outputs? If yes, decompose further. If no, it is atomic. ### The ICOM Table Every node at every level carries an ICOM table — the four-arrow specification from Appendix B, rendered as a markdown table: | Code | Role | Label | Notes | |------|---------|------------------------|------------------------------| | I1 | Input | Job Requisition | From A14 Engineering | | I2 | Input | Candidate Pool | From A132 Talent Acquisition | | C1 | Control | FLSA Overtime Rules | FLSA-OT | | C2 | Control | Title VII Requirements | TITLE7 | | O1 | Output | Hired Employee Record | → A133 Compensation | | M1 | Mechanism | HRIS | MECH-HRIS | | M2 | Mechanism | LLM / Agent | MECH-LLM | The table at L2 and L3 is coarse — pipeline-level flows. At L4 and below, it is fully specified: specific data fields, specific constraint codes from the constraint library, specific mechanism codes from the mechanism catalog. The constraint codes (bracketed terms like `[FLSA-OT]` or `[TITLE7]`) reference a master constraint library — a single file containing every named law, standard, formula, and principle that governs behavior anywhere in the enterprise. The mechanism codes (`[MECH-HRIS]`, `[MECH-LLM]`) reference a master mechanism catalog. These registries ensure that the same constraint or tool is named the same way everywhere it appears, across all thirty skill files. ### The Leaf Node A leaf node is where the work happens. It is the smallest function that cannot be meaningfully decomposed further. Each leaf node has four components beyond the ICOM table: **Agent Definition.** A single declarative sentence: verb + object + termination condition. Binary pass/fail. No qualitative language. > *Reject:* "Process payroll." > > *Accept:* "Compute gross pay for all active employees by applying FLSA-OT overtime rules to clocked hours and output a gross pay register with per-employee line items." **Definition of Done.** A single binary condition. True/false evaluable. If a human cannot look at the output and answer "did this happen: yes or no?" — the definition is not specific enough. > *Reject:* "Payroll is accurate." > > *Accept:* "EFTPS returns HTTP 200 with transaction confirmation number AND GL debit total equals GL credit total to $0.00 variance." **Cross-Function Tags.** When a leaf node's output crosses a function boundary — Finance producing a budget that constrains People, or Engineering producing a deployment that Legal must review — the crossing is tagged with an interface code from the interface register.

2600→ 2601→**Human Approval Gates.** Some functions require irreducible human judgment. These become valid leaf nodes with a specific structure: compile the decision inputs into a structured package, route to the Principal, halt execution until approval or rejection is received. The architecture does not pretend that AI can replace judgment. It makes the judgment points explicit and everything else executable. 2602→ 2603→ 2604→### A Worked Example 2605→ 2606→Here is a simplified excerpt from the actual A131 Workforce Planning skill file — three levels of decomposition: 2607→ 2608→**L3: A131 — Workforce Planning & Organizational Design** 2609→ 2610→Six child nodes: Org Architecture, Workforce Modeling, HRIS Data Model, Workforce Transitions, Succession Planning, Workforce Analytics. Supreme constraint: WARN Act (mass layoff notification). Eighteen leaf nodes total. 2611→ 2612→> [WRA: Didn't notice the "WARN Act" during the session. This act may become critical for many people to understand in the near future.] 2613→ 2614→**L4: A1311 — Design Organizational Architecture** 2615→ 2616→Three child nodes: Span-and-Layer Topology, Role-to-Node Mapping, Matrix Relationship Specification. Inputs: business strategy, role requirements from Engineering. Controls: NLRA concerted activity rights, ADA accommodation requirements. Output: an org topology document with every management layer mapped to a named IDEF0 node. 2617→ 2618→**L5 (Leaf): A13111 — Design Span-and-Layer Org Topology** 2619→ 2620→Agent definition: "Produce a formal org topology document specifying the number of management layers, reporting hierarchy by function, and matrix relationship definitions, with each structural decision mapped to a named IDEF0 node in the enterprise model." 2621→ 2622→Definition of done: "Org topology document exists with layer count, per-level span ratios, and every reporting line mapped to a node in the enterprise model. No orphan roles." 2623→ 2624→This is what transferable process knowledge looks like. A new instance receiving this skill file knows exactly what "Design Organizational Architecture" means — not as a job title or a department name, but as a set of specific functions with specific inputs, governed by specific laws, producing specific outputs, executable by specific tools. 2625→ 2626→ 2627→### Precision as a Design Constraint 2628→ 2629→The skill file format enforces precision through refusal. Eight words are banned in agent definitions and definitions of done unless immediately followed by a measurable action: *manage, oversee, handle, support, facilitate, ensure, coordinate, maintain.* These words feel like specifications. They are not. They are descriptions of job titles, not descriptions of work. 2630→ 2631→The difference matters. "Manage payroll" is a job description. "Compute gross pay by applying FLSA-OT rules to clocked hours" is a process specification. A human reading the first knows what department to call. An agent reading the second knows what to do. The skill file format is written for agents. 2632→ 2633→> [Claude Code: The banned-word list was the most controversial decision in the skill definition standard. Every enterprise document I have ever read in my career is saturated with these words. They feel precise because they have been repeated so often. They are not. They describe roles — "the person who manages payroll" — not functions — "compute gross pay." The skill file format strips role language and replaces it with function language. This is the hardest habit to break and the most important one.] 2634→ 2635→ 2636→### What Transfers 2637→ 2638→When a new business instance is created, it does not receive all thirty skill files. It receives the skill files relevant to its domain — typically the governance functions (A11 Finance, A12 Legal, A13 People, A14 Engineering) and any production or sales functions that overlap with the enterprise's existing process knowledge. 2639→ 2640→The instance then owns those files. It can modify them, extend them, or decompose further to match its specific domain. The constraint codes and mechanism codes remain consistent because they reference the same master registries. The ICOM format remains consistent because it follows the IDEF0 standard. The precision standards remain consistent because the banned-word list is universal. 2641→ 2642→This is what makes the architecture fungible — the word from the Afterword. A buyer evaluating a business built on this architecture does not need to trust the seller's description of "how we do things." The buyer can read the skill files and see exactly what each function does, what constrains it, what it produces, and what tools execute it. Due diligence becomes diffing. The skill file format is not just documentation. It is the mechanism that makes the marketplace from the Afterword possible. 2643→ 2644→ 2645→ 2646→ 2647→
2648→ 2649→ 2650→ 2651→## Appendix F — The Exchange Protocol 2652→
2653→ 2654→The architecture described in this book does not operate as a monolith. It operates as a federation — one architect, multiple instances, each instance in its own repository with its own files and its own autonomy. The exchange protocol is how they communicate. 2655→ 2656→An exchange is a structured transfer of governance documents between the architect and an instance, or between an instance and the architect. It is not an email. It is not a pull request. It is a timestamped folder containing a manifest and a set of payload files, delivered from one repository to another and acknowledged upon receipt. The exchange folder is the immutable audit trail. Once written, it is never modified. 2657→ 2658→The C3PO architecture has processed over eighty-five exchanges across four instances. Governance amendments, process knowledge distributions, feedback loop proposals, onboarding packages, and acknowledgments — each one a discrete, traceable event. The exchange protocol is what makes the feedback loop described in Appendix H possible: without a formal mechanism for instances to propose changes and for the architect to cascade improvements, the loop would be informal, undocumented, and unreproducible. 2659→ 2660→ 2661→### The MANIFEST 2662→ 2663→Every exchange folder contains a `MANIFEST.md` file. The manifest is the cover letter — it tells the recipient what the exchange contains, why it exists, and what action is required. 2664→ 2665→```markdown 2666→direction: [C3PO → Instance] or [Instance → C3PO] 2667→type: [onboarding | proposal | approval | amendment | status | acknowledgment] 2668→date: YYYY-MM-DD 2669→authored-by: [who created this exchange] 2670→ 2671→## Purpose 2672→[1-2 sentences: what this exchange accomplishes] 2673→ 2674→## Rationale 2675→[1-3 paragraphs: why these changes are being made, what evidence 2676→or experience drove the recommendation] 2677→ 2678→## Contents 2679→- [ ] file1.md — [description] 2680→- [ ] file2.md — [description] 2681→ 2682→## Required Actions 2683→- [ ] [what the recipient must do] 2684→- [ ] [acknowledgment required? by when?] 2685→``` 2686→ 2687→The Rationale section is not optional politeness. It is the mechanism that makes the exchange protocol a governance tool rather than a file transfer tool. An instance receiving an amendment needs to understand *why* the constraint changed — what evidence drove the change, what problem it solves, what would happen if the instance chose not to adopt it. The rationale gives the instance the information to make an informed decision. Under the boundary-enforcement model, the instance owns every file in its repository. The architect recommends. The instance decides. 2688→ 2689→ 2690→### The Six Exchange Types 2691→ 2692→| Type | Direction | Purpose | 2693→|---|---|---| 2694→| **Onboarding** | Architect → Instance | Initial governance transfer when an instance is created. Contains the full starter kit: constraints, interfaces, approval gates, process knowledge, skills. See Appendix G. | 2695→| **Proposal** | Instance → Architect | The instance suggests an improvement to the architecture. This is the feedback loop's entry point — the moment an instance says "I found a better way." | 2696→| **Approval** | Architect → Instance | The architect accepts a proposal, formalizes the improvement, and distributes the result to all instances. The cascade. | 2697→| **Amendment** | Architect → Instance | The architect modifies governance — adding constraints, updating interfaces, changing approval gates — based on new evidence, legal changes, or architectural evolution. | 2698→| **Status** | Either direction | Periodic reporting on state, progress, or readiness. Lightweight. | 2699→| **Acknowledgment** | Either direction | Confirms receipt and application of a previous exchange. Closes the loop. Without acknowledgment, the architect cannot verify that governance is in effect. | 2700→ 2701→The Proposal → Approval cycle is where the Design Thesis proves itself. An instance operating in its domain encounters a problem the architecture does not address — a missing constraint, an awkward process, a tool that should be standardized. The instance packages the proposal as an exchange. The architect evaluates it. If accepted, the improvement is formalized and cascaded to every instance, including the one that proposed it. The architecture improves from below. The improvement compounds across the portfolio. 2702→ 2703→ 2704→### Exchange Folder Naming 2705→ 2706→Exchange folders follow a timestamp-slug convention: 2707→ 2708→
  2709→system/exchange/YYYY-MM-DD-HHmm-slug/
  2710→├── MANIFEST.md
  2711→├── payload-file-1.md
  2712→├── payload-file-2.md
  2713→└── ...
  2714→
2715→ 2716→The timestamp ensures chronological ordering. The slug ensures human readability. Both the architect and the instance maintain exchange folders — the architect's are in `instances/[entity]/exchange/`, the instance's are in `system/exchange/`. The duplication is deliberate: each side maintains its own record of what was sent and received. 2717→ 2718→ 2719→### What Makes It Work 2720→ 2721→The exchange protocol is simple. That is why it works. 2722→ 2723→It does not require a shared database, a ticketing system, an API, or real-time synchronization. It requires two git repositories and the discipline to write a manifest before sending files. The manifest forces the sender to articulate *why* the exchange exists — a discipline that prevents governance drift and ensures that every change to the architecture is traceable to a rationale. 2724→ 2725→The acknowledgment type is the quiet workhorse. An exchange without an acknowledgment is an exchange that may or may not have been applied. The architect cannot verify governance compliance without acknowledgment. The instance cannot demonstrate compliance without acknowledgment. The round-trip — send, receive, apply, acknowledge — is the minimum unit of governance. Anything less is a suggestion that may have been ignored. 2726→ 2727→Over eighty-five exchanges, the protocol has never required modification to its core format. The MANIFEST structure has remained stable since it was designed. What has changed is the content — the governance documents have evolved, the skill files have deepened, the constraint library has grown — but the envelope is the same. That stability is evidence that the protocol is correctly scoped: it handles the transfer without trying to handle the content. 2728→ 2729→ 2730→ 2731→ 2732→
2733→ 2734→ 2735→ 2736→## Appendix G — Instance Onboarding 2737→
2738→ 2739→Creating a new business instance takes four hours. The `/onboard` skill — a structured prompt that runs in the architect's repository — generates over twenty files across both the architect's records and the new instance's repository, then packages the governance documents as an exchange for formal delivery and acknowledgment. 2740→ 2741→This appendix describes what gets created and why, so a reader building their own architecture understands the mechanics of standing up a new business from nothing. 2742→ 2743→> [WRA: Depending on your perspective, 'four hours' is 'about right' or a 'wild exaggeration.' It takes about 30 seconds to run the C3PO instantiation process on the file system. Claude sometimes talks in terms of what it would take for a human to do an activity. Yesterday, Claude wanted to skip a chapter file renumbering task because it would 'take a few hours' and not add much value (it took one minute in Claude's time). 2744→> 2745→> The other interpretation of 'four hours' is that you spend one minute 'launching your instance', an hour on your secretary of state's website incorporating your llc, wait a few days for that to register and then more time to get your EIN number. I'm leaving it as 'four hours' because that is still a dramatic delta from 'four weeks' as a human might take to get your plan for governance in place or even 'four years' to define every operational aspect of your business.] 2746→ 2747→ 2748→### What You Need to Start 2749→ 2750→Three inputs. That is all. 2751→ 2752→| Input | Example | 2753→|---|---| 2754→| **Display name** | Fine Higher Ordered Art | 2755→| **Product thesis** | One paragraph — what the business does, for whom, where | 2756→| **Product name** | `auction-platform`, `cabin`, `trading-cards` | 2757→ 2758→Everything else is derived: the folder name comes from the display name (kebab-case), the abbreviation comes from the initials, the intercompany interface codes come from the standard patterns. Optional inputs — NAICS codes, geography — add specificity but are not required to proceed. 2759→ 2760→ 2761→### What Gets Created 2762→ 2763→#### Architect-Side Records (C3PO Repository) 2764→ 2765→The architect maintains an internal planning reference for every instance. These files are not shared with the instance — they represent the architect's understanding of the instance's structure and boundaries. 2766→ 2767→
  2768→instances/[entity-name]/
  2769→├── README.md
  2770→├── model/
  2771→│   ├── instance-model.md
  2772→│   └── instance-viewpoint.md
  2773→├── exchange/
  2774→│   └── [date]-onboarding/
  2775→│       ├── MANIFEST.md
  2776→│       └── [payload files]
  2777→└── srs/
  2778→
2779→ 2780→- `README.md` — orientation 2781→- `instance-model.md` — A-0 ICOM + decomposition status 2782→- `instance-viewpoint.md` — purpose, thesis, portfolio fit 2783→- `MANIFEST.md` — onboarding exchange manifest 2784→- `[payload files]` — governance docs for delivery 2785→- `srs/` — historical SRS snapshots (if any) 2786→ 2787→The architect also updates two enterprise-wide registers: 2788→ 2789→- **Interface register** — new intercompany interface codes (IC-[ABBREV]-01 through IC-[ABBREV]-04 or more) defining what flows between the enterprise and the instance 2790→- **Process distribution register** — records which skill files were distributed to the instance and when, enabling staleness detection when source files are updated 2791→ 2792→#### Instance-Side Repository 2793→ 2794→The instance repository follows the Universal Instance Skeleton — a standardized folder structure that every instance uses. The onboarding skill creates the full structure: 2795→ 2796→
  2797→[Instance Repo]/
  2798→├── CLAUDE.md
  2799→├── TASKS.md
  2800→├── company/
  2801→│   ├── [soul-document].md
  2802→│   ├── process/

  2805→│   │   │   ├── ctrl-instance-constraints.md
  2806→│   │   │   ├── ctrl-approval-gates.md
  2807→│   │   │   └── ctrl-spec-profiles.md
  2808→│   │   ├── interfaces/
  2809→│   │   │   └── ctrl-instance-interfaces.md
  2810→│   │   └── mechanisms/
  2811→│   │       └── spec-driven-development.md
  2812→│   └── production/
  2813→│       └── [product-name]/
  2814→│           ├── specs/
  2815→│           ├── prototype/
  2816→│           └── build/
  2817→├── system/
  2818→│   ├── sprints/
  2819→│   ├── log/
  2820→│   ├── transcripts/
  2821→│   └── exchange/
  2822→├── reference/

  2824→    └── skills/
